original_abstract: Zinc metal batteries are strongly hindered by water corrosion, as solvated zinc ions would bring the active water molecules to the electrode/electrolyte interface constantly. Herein, we report a sacrificial solvation shell to repel active water molecules from the electrode/electrolyte interface and assist in forming a fluoride-rich, organic-inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer. The simultaneous sacrificial process of methanol and Zn(CF<sub>3</sub>SO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub> results in the gradient SEI layer with an organic-rich surface (CH<sub>2</sub>OC- and C<sub>5</sub> product) and an inorganic-rich (ZnF<sub>2</sub>) bottom, which combines the merits of fast ion diffusion and high flexibility. As a result, the methanol additive enables corrosion-free zinc stripping/plating on copper foils for 300 cycles with an average coulombic efficiency of 99.5%, a record high cumulative plating capacity of 10 A h/cm<sup>2</sup> at 40 mA/cm<sup>2</sup> in Zn/Zn symmetrical batteries. More importantly, at an ultralow N/P ratio of 2, the practical VO<sub>2</sub>//20 μm thick Zn plate full batteries with a high areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm<sup>2</sup> stably operate for over 250 cycles, establishing their promising application for grid-scale energy storage devices. Furthermore, directly utilizing the 20 μm thick Zn for the commercial-level areal capacity (4.7 mAh/cm<sup>2</sup>) full zinc battery in our work would simplify the manufacturing process and boost the development of the commercial zinc battery for stationary storage.
humanized_abstract: Our previous studies demonstrated that water corrosion severely obstructs the development of zinc metal batteries due to the continuous introduction of solvated zinc ions and accompanying water molecules to the electrode/electrolyte interface. In this work, we for the first time design a sacrificial solvation shell that not only repels the active water molecules away from the electrode/electrode interface but also acts as a catalyst for the formation of a fluoride-rich, organic-inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interface (SEI) layer. The simultaneous sacrificial reactions of methanol and Zn(CF3SO3)2 lead to the formation of a gradient SEI layer consisting of an organic-inorganic gradient solid electrolyte interface with an organic-rich surface layer (composed of CH2OC- and C5 product) and an inorganic-rich bottom layer (ZnF2). Thus, our work provides an innovative strategy to prevent the water corrosion of zinc batteries, and our zinc batteries show the corrosion-free zinc stripping/plating on copper foils for 300 cycles with an average coulombic efficiency of 99.5% and a record high cumulative plating capacity of 10 A h/cm2 at 40 mA/cm2 in Zn/Zn symmetrical batteries. Notably, at an ultralow N/P ratio of 2, the practical VO2//20 μm thick Zn plate full batteries with a high areal capacity of 4.7 mAh/cm2 can stably operate for over 250 cycles, which opens up new opportunities for the use of zinc batteries in the field of grid-scale energy storage. In addition, directly utilizing the 20 μm thick Zn for the commercial-level areal capacity (4.7 mAh/cm2) full zinc battery in our work would greatly simplify the fabrication process and thus promote the commercialization of the stationary zinc battery.

Thus, our work provides an innovative strategy to prevent the water corrosion of zinc batteries, and our zinc batteries show the corrosion-free zinc stripping/plating on copper foils for 300 cycles with an average coulombic efficiency of 99.5% and a record high cumulative plating capacity of 10 A h/cm2 at 40 mA/cm2 in Zn/Zn symmetrical batteries.
